system_context.business_goals: To achieve a blend which isolates critical functionality, flexible control, and user support while meeting the needs of the broadest collection of serious users.

architectures.name: WinSAAM Hybrid Architecture
architectures.description: A hybrid architecture that wraps a legacy FORTRAN-based computational kernel (SAAM/Consam) with modern Windows-based interface components. The system uses a component-based approach where specialized modules (spreadsheet, chart manager, file manager) communicate through a central Interface/Terminal component that coordinates activities and manages the legacy kernel. Third-party DLLs and dynamic linking enable integration of modern UI capabilities while preserving proven computational algorithms.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Modular component design with clear separation of concerns; Backward compatibility with legacy Consam workflows; Extensibility through domain libraries; User-facing flexibility through multiple input/editing modes (CLI, text editor, spreadsheet); Maintainability constrained by legacy kernel dependency; Performance dependent on kernel processing capabilities

key_components.responsibilities: Acts as the supervisory hub and orchestration layer. Provides CLI-style command processing, terminal input/output, and coordinates communication between all other components. Manages user sessions and controls processing workflow.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Central coordination point; changes here ripple to all components

key_components.responsibilities: Project management and assembly. Combines input components into cohesive project specifications, manages project lifecycle (create, edit, save, load), and prepares data structures for kernel processing.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Changes affect project structure and kernel input format

key_components.responsibilities: Provides line-oriented text editing capabilities for direct manipulation of model specifications and parameters. Manages graphic text representation of models.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Relatively isolated; can be enhanced or replaced without major system impact

key_components.responsibilities: Tabular data input, manipulation, and output. Provides familiar spreadsheet interface for data entry, parameter specification, and results formatting. Alternative input method to text-based editing.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low-Medium - Isolated input/output component; changes affect user workflow but not core processing

key_components.responsibilities: Centralized file operations coordinator. Manages all user file access, ensures file integrity, handles file format conversions, and maintains file system organization for projects, data, and results.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Changes affect all components that persist or retrieve data; file format changes require coordination

key_components.responsibilities: Visualization and plotting engine. Generates charts from results data, provides plot configuration and customization, enables chart-based user communication and result interpretation.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Presentation layer component; can be enhanced independently; depends on stable data format from kernel

key_components.responsibilities: Core computational engine (legacy FORTRAN code). Performs kinetic modeling calculations, system solving, iterative parameter adjustment, and numerical optimization. Contains proven algorithms from decades of research use.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Very High - Legacy code; changes are risky and expensive; requires specialized knowledge; affects scientific validity of results

key_components.responsibilities: Extensible repository of pre-built models for specific research domains (e.g., lipid metabolism, glucose kinetics). Provides templates and starting points for domain experts. Enables community contribution of validated models.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Designed for extensibility; new libraries can be added without system changes; library format changes affect kernel compatibility

architectures.data_flow: User initiates work through the Interface/Terminal Window using either CLI commands, the Editor for text-based model specification, or the Spreadsheet for tabular data entry. The Linker assembles these inputs into a complete project specification, coordinating with the FileManager to retrieve/store project files. When ready for computation, the Interface passes the assembled specification to the SAAM/Consam Kernel, which performs calculations and returns results. Results flow back through the Interface to either the Spreadsheet (for tabular output), ChartManager (for visualization), or Editor (for text reports). The FileManager handles all persistence operations. Domain Libraries provide pre-configured model templates that flow through the same pathway. All components communicate through the central Interface hub rather than directly with each other.
architectural_constraints: Legacy kernel dependency: The SAAM/Consam kernel is written in FORTRAN and cannot be easily modified or replaced without significant risk to scientific validity
Windows platform dependency: Architecture assumes Windows OS and Windows-specific DLLs
Single-user desktop application: Not designed for multi-user, concurrent access, or web-based deployment
Synchronous processing model: Kernel processing blocks the interface; no background computation support evident
File-based data exchange: Components communicate through file I/O rather than in-memory data structures, limiting performance
Backward compatibility requirement: Must support legacy Consam workflows and file formats
Hub-and-spoke communication: All components route through the Interface, creating a potential bottleneck and single point of failure

easily_accommodated: Adding new domain libraries without system changes
Improving the Editor with modern text editing features
Replacing or upgrading the Spreadsheet component with commercial alternatives
Adding new file format support in FileManager
Extending CLI commands in the Interface for workflow automation
Adding validation and integrity checks in the Linker
Supporting additional chart types and export formats
moderately_difficult: Changing project file formats (affects Linker, FileManager, and all input components)
Adding real-time collaboration features (requires architectural changes to file-based model)
Implementing undo/redo across components (no evident transaction management)
Adding background processing (requires threading model changes in Interface)
Migrating to cross-platform support (Windows dependencies throughout)
Implementing a plugin architecture for third-party extensions
Adding comprehensive audit logging across components
very_difficult_or_not_supported: Web-based or cloud deployment (desktop architecture assumptions)
Multi-user concurrent editing of same project (single-user design)
Modifying core computational algorithms (legacy kernel constraint)
Microservices decomposition (tightly coupled through Interface hub)
Replacing the kernel with alternative computational engines (deep integration)
Distributed computing or parallel processing (kernel is monolithic)

scenario_1.change: Add support for a new model domain (e.g., bone metabolism)
scenario_1.explanation: Domain Libraries are designed for extension. New library files can be added and registered with minimal system impact.

scenario_2.change: Replace text editor with modern syntax-highlighting editor
scenario_2.explanation: Editor is relatively isolated. Main impact is ensuring Interface can properly invoke new editor and that file format compatibility is maintained.

scenario_3.change: Enable background kernel processing with progress updates
scenario_3.explanation: Requires fundamental changes to synchronous processing model. Interface must support threading, kernel must be wrapped for async invocation, and all components must handle concurrent state.

scenario_4.explanation: Windows-specific DLLs, UI frameworks, and file system assumptions permeate the architecture. Kernel may require recompilation. Essentially a re-architecture effort.

scenario_5.change: Add web-based interface while keeping kernel
scenario_5.explanation: Requires replacing entire presentation layer and rethinking file-based communication model. Only Kernel and possibly FileManager could be preserved.

architectures.notes: This architecture represents a pragmatic 'wrapper' approach to modernizing legacy scientific software. The strategy preserves validated computational algorithms (critical for scientific credibility) while improving user experience through modern Windows UI components. The hub-and-spoke pattern around the Interface component creates both a strength (clear coordination) and a weakness (potential bottleneck, tight coupling). The file-based communication between components suggests this may have evolved from separate executables being integrated. Stakeholders should understand that this architecture prioritizes backward compatibility and computational integrity over modern architectural qualities like scalability, cloud-readiness, or multi-user support. Future evolution is constrained by the legacy kernel - any strategic direction requiring kernel modification or replacement would be extremely costly and risky.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user researcher loads a previously saved kinetic model file, modifies parameter values in the spreadsheet interface, executes the SAAM simulation, and views results in graphical charts within 5 minutes without requiring command-line interaction.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by current architecture. The component-based design with File Manager for loading, Spreadsheet for parameter editing, Kernel for computation, and Chart Manager for visualization aligns perfectly with this workflow.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Core use case for nutritional researchers who need intuitive, GUI-driven access to kinetic modeling without FORTRAN/command-line expertise. Represents the primary value proposition of the Windows migration.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to integrate a new third-party statistical analysis library (DLL) into WinSAAM to extend post-processing capabilities, completing the integration in 2 developer-days without modifying the SAAM/Consam Kernel code.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported. The architecture mentions 'dynamic linking' and 'third-party DLLs' suggesting extensibility, but the degree of isolation and API clarity for adding new libraries is uncertain. The Linker component suggests capability, but integration patterns need validation.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Ensures the system can evolve with new analytical methods in nutritional research without destabilizing the proven computational core. Critical for long-term relevance and meeting 'broadest collection of serious users' needs.

scenarios.scenario_text: When the SAAM/Consam Kernel encounters a computational error during simulation, the system logs detailed diagnostic information, displays a user-friendly error message through the Interface/Terminal Window, and allows the user to modify inputs and retry without restarting the application.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Requires robust error handling bridge between legacy FORTRAN kernel and modern UI. Current architecture shows separation but doesn't explicitly describe error propagation mechanisms. May require enhancement to kernel wrapper layer.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for user support and productivity. Researchers need to understand and recover from modeling errors without technical assistance, aligning with 'user support' and 'flexible control' goals.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system maintainer applies a bug fix to the Chart Manager component and deploys the updated module to production systems without requiring recompilation of the SAAM/Consam Kernel or other interface components, completing deployment in under 1 hour.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by component-based architecture with dynamic linking. The modular design should enable independent component updates, though deployment procedures and version compatibility management need to be established.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Demonstrates architectural goal of 'isolating critical functionality' - ability to maintain and update UI/support components without risking the computational core. Reduces maintenance risk and downtime.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator installs WinSAAM on 50 Windows workstations across a research facility using automated deployment tools, with each installation completing in under 10 minutes and requiring no manual configuration of component dependencies.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Depends on packaging strategy not explicitly described in architecture. Component-based design with DLLs can support this if proper dependency management and installation scripts are implemented. Third-party DLL licensing and distribution rights may be constraints.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Enables broad deployment to 'broadest collection of serious users' across institutional settings. Reduces total cost of ownership and administrative burden for research organizations.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user exports simulation results from the Spreadsheet component to Excel format and chart visualizations to PNG/PDF formats for inclusion in research publications, completing the export in under 2 minutes with publication-quality output.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Likely supported given modern Windows components, but export format quality and compatibility depend on third-party DLL capabilities. File Manager should coordinate export operations across components.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Essential for research workflow - results must integrate into publication pipelines. Supports 'user support' goal by enabling seamless transition from analysis to dissemination.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer modifies the Editor component to add syntax highlighting for SAAM model definition language, testing and integrating the change within 3 developer-days without impacting the Kernel's model parsing logic.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Supported by component isolation if Editor and Kernel have well-defined interfaces. Success depends on whether model text parsing is cleanly separated between UI (Editor) and computational (Kernel) concerns.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Enhances user experience for advanced users who directly edit model definitions. Demonstrates architectural flexibility for incremental UI improvements without kernel changes.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer upgrades the underlying FORTRAN compiler for the SAAM/Consam Kernel to address security vulnerabilities, recompiling the kernel and verifying compatibility with all interface components within 1 week, with no changes required to component interfaces.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Challenging - depends on stability of kernel interface contracts and whether FORTRAN runtime dependencies are properly abstracted. The 'wrapper' approach mentioned should isolate this, but binary compatibility and calling conventions may be fragile.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Long-term sustainability concern. Legacy FORTRAN code requires ongoing compiler/runtime support. Architecture must enable kernel maintenance without cascading changes to modern components.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user working with sensitive patient nutritional data configures WinSAAM to encrypt all saved model files and simulation results, with encryption/decryption transparent during normal operations and adding no more than 10% overhead to file I/O operations.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Not directly supported by current architecture description. Would require enhancement to File Manager component. Feasible as localized change if file I/O is properly centralized through File Manager rather than scattered across components.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Increasingly critical for clinical/human subjects research compliance (HIPAA, GDPR). Addresses 'serious users' in regulated research environments. Represents growth need for broader user base.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator monitors WinSAAM usage across a multi-user research server, tracking which users are running simulations, resource consumption per session, and generating monthly usage reports without requiring instrumentation code in the SAAM/Consam Kernel.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Not currently supported - would require adding telemetry/logging infrastructure. Could be implemented in Interface/Terminal Window as coordination point, but requires architectural decision on logging strategy and privacy considerations.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Supports resource planning and license compliance in institutional settings. Exploratory because it represents operational management capabilities beyond core research functionality.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer adds support for a new kinetic model type (e.g., fractional absorption models) by creating a new domain library that extends the system's modeling capabilities, integrating it within 2 weeks without modifying the core SAAM/Consam Kernel algorithms.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported - architecture mentions 'Domain Libraries' suggesting extensibility mechanism. Success depends on whether kernel provides plugin/extension points or if domain libraries are tightly coupled to kernel internals. Extension architecture not fully specified.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for meeting 'broadest collection of serious users' - different research domains need specialized models. Demonstrates whether architecture achieves 'flexible control' goal through extensibility.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer diagnoses a performance regression where simulations take 3x longer than expected by using profiling tools to identify bottlenecks across the component stack, isolating the issue to a specific component within 4 hours without access to original FORTRAN developers.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Challenging - requires observable component boundaries and performance instrumentation. Hybrid architecture mixing FORTRAN kernel with Windows components may complicate profiling. Component isolation helps, but cross-boundary performance analysis needs tooling support.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Long-term maintainability concern given legacy kernel. System must be diagnosable by current maintainers without deep FORTRAN expertise. Supports sustainability of 'critical functionality' isolation.

scenario_classification.rationale: This scenario represents the core use case that the architecture was explicitly designed to support. The component-based design with FileManager for loading files, Spreadsheet for parameter editing, Kernel for computation, and ChartManager for visualization directly enables this workflow. The Interface/Terminal Window orchestrates the entire flow. The 5-minute performance target is reasonable for desktop computation.

While the architecture mentions 'third-party DLLs' and 'dynamic linking', there is no explicit extension API or plugin architecture described. The Linker component handles project assembly, but its role in integrating new statistical libraries is unclear. The architecture would need defined extension points, API contracts, and integration patterns to reliably support this in 2 developer-days. Current design may require custom integration work for each new library.

The architecture describes component separation but does not specify error handling mechanisms between the legacy FORTRAN kernel and modern UI components. FORTRAN error handling (likely using error codes or STOP statements) needs translation to user-friendly messages. The architecture lacks explicit error propagation pathways, logging infrastructure, or recovery mechanisms. This requires a wrapper layer around the Kernel with structured error handling.

The component-based architecture with dynamic linking explicitly supports independent component updates. ChartManager has low change sensitivity and minimal dependencies (only FileManager). The modular design allows replacing or updating ChartManager DLL without recompiling other components. The 1-hour deployment target is achievable with proper version management and deployment procedures.

While the component-based architecture theoretically supports automated deployment, the architecture description lacks details on packaging, dependency management, installation procedures, and third-party DLL licensing/distribution. The 10-minute installation target requires pre-configured installers, automated dependency resolution, and registry/configuration management not explicitly described. Third-party DLL redistribution rights may be a constraint.

Modern Windows components (Spreadsheet and ChartManager) should inherently support standard export formats. The FileManager coordinates file operations. Excel export from spreadsheet components and PNG/PDF export from charting libraries are standard capabilities of third-party Windows DLLs. The 2-minute performance target is reasonable for file export operations.

The Editor component has low change sensitivity and is relatively isolated. Adding syntax highlighting is a UI enhancement that doesn't affect the Kernel's parsing logic if there's proper separation between text editing (Editor) and model interpretation (Kernel). The 3-day estimate is reasonable for integrating a syntax highlighting library into the Editor component without changing interfaces.

While the wrapper architecture should isolate the Kernel, FORTRAN compiler upgrades can introduce binary compatibility issues, calling convention changes, and runtime library dependencies. The architecture doesn't specify the interface contract between the Kernel and wrapper layer. FORTRAN-to-C/Windows interop can be fragile across compiler versions. The 1-week timeline is optimistic without explicit interface stability guarantees and comprehensive regression testing.

The architecture describes file-based communication but doesn't mention security features or encryption capabilities. Adding transparent encryption requires enhancing the FileManager component with encryption/decryption logic. The architecture doesn't specify whether file I/O is truly centralized through FileManager or if components directly access files. If file access is scattered, encryption becomes more complex. The 10% performance overhead constraint requires efficient implementation.

The architecture is described as a 'single-user desktop application' without multi-user server capabilities or telemetry infrastructure. Adding usage monitoring requires architectural changes for session tracking, resource monitoring, and reporting. The Interface component could be instrumented as the coordination point, but this represents new cross-cutting functionality not in the current design. Privacy and data collection policies need consideration.

Domain Libraries are mentioned as 'extensible repository' but the extension mechanism is not fully specified. The scenario requires adding new model types, which may need Kernel support for new algorithms or may be achievable through parameterization of existing algorithms. The architecture states Domain Libraries 'provide templates and starting points' but doesn't clarify if they can extend computational capabilities or just provide pre-configured models. The 2-week timeline depends on whether this is data-driven extension or requires Kernel modification.

The hybrid architecture mixing legacy FORTRAN with modern Windows components creates profiling challenges. Standard Windows profiling tools may not provide visibility into FORTRAN kernel internals. Component boundaries need instrumentation for performance measurement. The architecture doesn't describe performance monitoring capabilities, logging points, or diagnostic interfaces. The 4-hour diagnosis timeline requires pre-existing instrumentation and observable component interactions.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. This is the primary use case the architecture was designed to support.

Define and implement a plugin/extension API that allows third-party DLLs to register with the system. Create integration points in the Linker for post-processing hooks. Establish interface contracts for data exchange between statistical libraries and core components. Implement a registry mechanism for discovering and loading extension DLLs. Create documentation and sample code for extension developers.
issues_identified: No explicit plugin architecture or extension API defined
Unclear how third-party libraries access simulation results
No standardized data exchange format for extensions
Potential version compatibility issues between extensions and core system
Security concerns with loading arbitrary DLLs
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Create a post-processing pipeline in the Linker that accepts registered callback functions from DLLs. 2) Use the FileManager to export results to standard formats that external tools can process independently. 3) Implement a scripting interface in the Interface component that allows external programs to invoke statistical analysis on exported data.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement a Kernel wrapper layer that intercepts FORTRAN error conditions (error codes, exceptions, abnormal termination) and translates them to structured error objects. Create an error handling framework in the Interface component that receives error objects, logs diagnostic information, and displays user-friendly messages. Implement error recovery mechanisms that preserve user input state and allow retry without application restart. Add error logging infrastructure with configurable verbosity levels.
issues_identified: FORTRAN error handling may use legacy mechanisms (STOP statements, error codes) that are difficult to intercept
No explicit error propagation pathway from Kernel to UI components
State management for error recovery not described
Diagnostic information from FORTRAN code may be limited or cryptic
User input state preservation across error conditions not addressed
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Implement a process isolation approach where Kernel runs in separate process, allowing crash recovery without affecting UI. 2) Create comprehensive input validation in Linker to catch errors before Kernel execution. 3) Implement checkpoint/restore functionality that saves state before Kernel invocation, enabling rollback on error.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No architectural changes required. Establish deployment procedures and version management practices.
issues_identified: Need to establish version compatibility matrix for components
Deployment procedures and rollback mechanisms should be documented
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - Direct support exists. Consider implementing automated deployment scripts and version checking to ensure compatibility.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Create a comprehensive installation package that bundles all components, third-party DLLs, and dependencies. Implement automated dependency resolution and registration. Create installation scripts that configure file paths, registry entries, and component registration. Address third-party DLL licensing and redistribution rights. Implement silent installation mode for automated deployment. Create uninstall and upgrade procedures.
issues_identified: Third-party DLL licensing may restrict redistribution
Dependency management not explicitly described in architecture
Configuration requirements (registry, file paths) not specified
Version compatibility across 50 installations needs management
Network deployment and centralized configuration not addressed
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Use Windows Installer (MSI) technology with merge modules for components. 2) Implement a bootstrap installer that downloads components on-demand. 3) Create a portable installation that doesn't require registry modifications. 4) Use containerization (though this conflicts with Windows desktop architecture).

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required if third-party spreadsheet and charting DLLs support standard export formats. May need minor configuration or API calls to enable export functionality.
issues_identified: Publication-quality output depends on third-party DLL capabilities
Export format compatibility with specific Excel/image viewer versions may need testing
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - Direct support likely exists. If third-party components lack export capabilities, consider: 1) Switching to alternative DLLs with better export support. 2) Implementing custom export routines in FileManager. 3) Using Windows clipboard for data transfer.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Integrate syntax highlighting library into Editor component. Ensure text format passed to Kernel remains unchanged.
issues_identified: Need to verify Editor-to-Kernel interface is text-based and format-agnostic
Syntax highlighting rules need to be defined for SAAM model language
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: N/A - Direct support exists. Alternative: Replace entire Editor component with modern text editor control that includes syntax highlighting.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Define explicit interface contract between Kernel and wrapper layer using stable calling conventions (e.g., C-compatible interface). Implement comprehensive regression test suite covering all Kernel functionality. Create build procedures that verify binary compatibility. Document FORTRAN compiler dependencies and runtime requirements. Implement version checking to detect incompatibilities. Consider creating a C wrapper around FORTRAN code to stabilize interface.
issues_identified: FORTRAN compiler changes can affect binary compatibility, calling conventions, and runtime libraries
Interface contract between Kernel and Windows components not explicitly specified
Regression testing requirements for scientific code are extensive
FORTRAN-to-C/Windows interop can be fragile
Original FORTRAN developers may not be available for consultation
Scientific validation required after compiler changes
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Create a stable C wrapper around FORTRAN Kernel that isolates compiler dependencies. 2) Use FORTRAN-to-C translation tools to migrate away from FORTRAN dependency. 3) Maintain multiple compiler versions and test compatibility before upgrading. 4) Implement Kernel as separate executable with IPC, isolating compiler dependencies.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Enhance FileManager with encryption/decryption layer using Windows CryptoAPI or similar. Implement transparent encryption for all file I/O operations. Add key management functionality (password-based or certificate-based). Ensure all components route file operations through FileManager rather than direct file access. Implement configuration UI for encryption settings. Add performance optimization to meet 10% overhead constraint (e.g., streaming encryption, caching).
issues_identified: Architecture doesn't specify if all file I/O is centralized through FileManager
Components may directly access files, bypassing FileManager
Key management and password handling not addressed
Performance overhead constraint requires efficient implementation
Backward compatibility with unencrypted files needed
Compliance requirements (HIPAA, GDPR) may have specific encryption standards
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Use Windows file system encryption (EFS) at OS level, transparent to application. 2) Implement encrypted file containers that hold all project files. 3) Use database with encryption instead of file-based storage. 4) Implement application-level access controls without encryption.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: This scenario conflicts with the 'single-user desktop application' architecture. To support multi-user server monitoring: 1) Implement session management in Interface component to track user sessions. 2) Add resource monitoring hooks around Kernel invocations. 3) Create logging infrastructure that captures usage events. 4) Implement reporting module that aggregates usage data. 5) Address privacy concerns with user activity tracking. Alternatively, redesign as client-server architecture.
issues_identified: Architecture is explicitly single-user desktop, not multi-user server
No session management or user tracking capabilities
No telemetry or monitoring infrastructure described
Privacy concerns with user activity monitoring
Resource consumption tracking requires OS-level integration
Reporting infrastructure not present
Fundamental architecture mismatch with scenario requirements
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Use OS-level monitoring tools (Windows Performance Monitor, Event Logs) external to application. 2) Implement file-based usage logging that external tools can aggregate. 3) Create a separate monitoring service that observes WinSAAM processes. 4) Redesign as client-server architecture if multi-user server deployment is required.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Clarify and implement Domain Library extension mechanism. If new model types require Kernel algorithm changes, this becomes high-risk Kernel modification. If achievable through parameterization: 1) Define domain library format and schema. 2) Implement library loading and registration in Linker. 3) Create model template system that Kernel can interpret. 4) Develop library authoring tools and documentation. 5) Implement validation for new libraries. The 2-week timeline assumes no Kernel changes required.
issues_identified: Domain Library extension mechanism not fully specified
Unclear if new model types require Kernel algorithm changes
Distinction between 'templates' and 'new capabilities' ambiguous
Kernel extensibility for new model types uncertain
Validation and testing requirements for new models not addressed
Scientific validation of new model types required
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) If Kernel modification required, extend Kernel with plugin architecture (high risk). 2) Implement model types as parameterized configurations of existing algorithms. 3) Create domain-specific preprocessors that transform new models to existing Kernel formats. 4) Use scripting/macro language to compose existing Kernel capabilities into new model types.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement performance instrumentation framework: 1) Add timing instrumentation at component boundaries in Interface. 2) Create logging infrastructure for performance metrics. 3) Implement Kernel wrapper that measures execution time and resource usage. 4) Add performance monitoring hooks in file I/O operations (FileManager). 5) Create diagnostic mode that enables detailed profiling. 6) Develop analysis tools for performance data. Address FORTRAN profiling challenges with external tools or instrumented wrapper.
issues_identified: Hybrid FORTRAN/Windows architecture complicates profiling
Standard Windows profilers may not provide FORTRAN kernel visibility
Component boundaries need instrumentation for measurement
No performance monitoring infrastructure described
File-based communication between components may obscure bottlenecks
4-hour diagnosis timeline requires pre-existing instrumentation
FORTRAN expertise may be needed to interpret Kernel performance issues
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: 1) Use external profiling tools (Intel VTune, Visual Studio Profiler) that support mixed-language applications. 2) Implement process-level monitoring that measures component execution time without internal instrumentation. 3) Add comprehensive logging with timestamps at component boundaries. 4) Create performance test suite with known baselines for regression detection. 5) Implement sampling-based profiling that periodically captures system state.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: Interface is the most heavily impacted component (7 scenarios) and serves as the critical integration point between users and the system. Affects error handling (S-03), extensibility (S-02, S-11), security (S-09), monitoring (S-10, S-12), and compiler upgrades (S-08). Failure to address creates poor user experience during errors, blocks third-party integrations, prevents security compliance, and complicates maintenance. All stakeholder roles depend on this component functioning correctly.

Kernel is the scientific computation core with high-risk scenarios. S-08 (compiler upgrade) poses HIGH risk to system stability and scientific validity. S-03 (error handling) affects user trust when calculations fail. S-11 (new models) determines system extensibility. S-12 (performance diagnosis) is critical for maintaining acceptable response times. FORTRAN legacy code makes changes risky and requires specialized expertise. Kernel failures directly impact scientific accuracy and user productivity.

Linker serves as the critical integration layer between FORTRAN Kernel and Windows components (5 scenarios). S-08 (compiler upgrade) requires stable interface contracts through Linker. S-02 (plugin API) and S-11 (new models) depend on Linker's extension mechanisms. S-05 (installation) requires proper component registration. S-03 (error handling) needs error propagation pathways. Linker failures create fragile component coupling, deployment issues, and block extensibility—all high-impact for long-term maintainability.

FileManager impacts security (S-09 encryption - MEDIUM risk), deployment (S-05), extensibility (S-02), and monitoring (S-10). S-09 is critical for compliance (HIPAA/GDPR) but localized to FileManager. Architecture uncertainty exists around whether all file I/O is centralized—if components bypass FileManager, security and monitoring fail. S-05 affects 50-installation deployment. Medium priority because issues are contained but have compliance and operational consequences.

Cross-cutting concerns affecting entire system: S-05 (installation/deployment) impacts 50-user rollout with widespread changes needed for packaging, dependency management, and licensing. S-12 (performance diagnosis) requires instrumentation across all component boundaries. Both scenarios have MEDIUM effort and risk but are operationally critical. Installation issues block adoption; performance issues affect user satisfaction. Primarily impacts operational and maintenance roles rather than end-users directly.

DomainLibs affects only 2 scenarios: S-11 (new model types) and S-05 (installation/licensing). S-11 has ambiguous requirements—if achievable through parameterization, risk is MEDIUM and localized. If requires Kernel changes, becomes HIGH risk but shifts to Kernel component. S-05 licensing concerns are administrative rather than technical. Lower priority because: (1) extension mechanism is unclear in architecture, (2) impacts specialized users needing new models rather than all users, (3) workarounds exist through external tools or Kernel modification.

architectural_strengths: Strength 1: Strong support for core scientific workflow - The architecture directly supports primary use cases (S-01, S-04, S-06, S-07) with no or minimal changes required. The component separation between Editor, Kernel, and visualization tools aligns well with the scientific modeling workflow, enabling users to edit models, run simulations, and visualize results without architectural modifications.

Strength 2: Component-based modularity enables localized enhancements - Several scenarios (S-02, S-07, S-08, S-09) can be addressed with localized changes to specific components. The separation of concerns between Interface, FileManager, Editor, and Kernel allows targeted improvements like syntax highlighting (S-07) or encryption (S-09) without cascading changes across the entire system.

Strength 3: Proven stability for single-user desktop deployment - The architecture successfully supports the intended single-user Windows desktop environment, as evidenced by direct support for deployment scenarios (S-04) and publication-quality output (S-06). The Windows-native approach with third-party DLLs for spreadsheet/charting provides familiar user experience with low risk.

Strength 4: Clear separation between scientific computation and UI concerns - The Kernel component isolation protects the validated FORTRAN scientific code from UI changes. This separation allows UI enhancements (S-07 syntax highlighting) and file management improvements (S-09 encryption) without risking scientific accuracy or requiring re-validation of computational algorithms.
architectural_weaknesses: Weakness 1: Fragile FORTRAN-Windows integration creates high-risk maintenance burden - The Kernel component's FORTRAN implementation creates significant challenges across 5 scenarios (S-03, S-08, S-10, S-11, S-12). S-08 (compiler upgrade) is HIGH risk due to binary compatibility concerns, calling convention fragility, and lack of explicit interface contracts. Error handling (S-03) struggles with legacy FORTRAN mechanisms (STOP statements, error codes), and performance diagnosis (S-12) is complicated by hybrid language profiling. This architectural decision creates long-term technical debt and requires specialized expertise that may not be available.

Weakness 2: Missing extensibility infrastructure blocks third-party integration and evolution - The architecture lacks explicit plugin/extension mechanisms needed for S-02 (statistical libraries), S-11 (new model types), and potentially S-09 (encryption). No standardized API contracts, data exchange formats, or registration mechanisms are defined. This forces medium-effort, widespread changes for scenarios that should be routine extensions, limiting the system's ability to evolve with user needs and integrate with the broader scientific software ecosystem.

Weakness 3: Unclear component boundaries and centralization create security and monitoring gaps - Critical architectural details are underspecified: whether all file I/O is centralized through FileManager (S-09 encryption fails if bypassed), how components communicate beyond file-based exchange (complicates S-10 monitoring and S-12 performance diagnosis), and whether session/resource tracking exists (S-10 fundamentally unsupported). These ambiguities create risk that localized changes won't achieve their goals because components may bypass intended control points.

Weakness 4: Single-user desktop architecture fundamentally misaligned with emerging requirements - S-10 (multi-user server monitoring) exposes a HIGH-risk architectural mismatch requiring widespread changes with high effort. The architecture has no session management, user tracking, telemetry infrastructure, or resource monitoring capabilities. This suggests the architecture may not scale to evolving deployment models (cloud, multi-tenant, enterprise management) without fundamental redesign.

Weakness 5: Inadequate error handling and diagnostic infrastructure - Error propagation pathways from Kernel to UI are not explicitly defined (S-03), performance instrumentation is absent (S-12), and state management for error recovery is unaddressed (S-03). These gaps affect 7 scenarios and create poor user experience during failures, extended diagnosis times (4-hour target in S-12 requires pre-existing instrumentation), and potential data loss. The architecture treats error handling and observability as afterthoughts rather than first-class concerns.

Weakness 6: Deployment and dependency management not architected for scale - S-05 (50-installation deployment) reveals widespread issues: no dependency resolution mechanism, unclear configuration requirements (registry, file paths), third-party DLL licensing restrictions not addressed, and no centralized management for version compatibility. These gaps create medium-effort deployment challenges and operational risk across the user base, suggesting the architecture was designed for small-scale, manual deployment rather than enterprise distribution.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture demonstrates moderate modifiability for UI and file management enhancements but poor modifiability for extensibility, error handling, and cross-cutting concerns. The FORTRAN Kernel creates a high-risk, low-modifiability core that constrains evolution, while missing extension APIs and unclear component boundaries force widespread changes for scenarios that should be localized. Overall, the architecture supports incremental improvements to existing functionality but struggles with new capabilities, integration scenarios, and operational requirements.

recommendations.recommendation: Define and implement stable interface contracts between Kernel and Windows components with explicit C-compatible wrapper layer
recommendations.rationale: Addresses the highest-risk scenario (S-08 compiler upgrade) and improves 5 scenarios (S-03, S-08, S-10, S-11, S-12) by isolating FORTRAN dependencies, enabling error interception, facilitating performance monitoring, and creating extension points. A stable C wrapper protects against compiler changes, provides hooks for error handling and instrumentation, and establishes clear boundaries for future modifications. This is foundational for long-term maintainability and reduces risk of scientific code invalidation.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement comprehensive error handling framework with explicit propagation pathways from Kernel through Linker to Interface
recommendations.rationale: Affects S-03 directly and improves user experience across all scenarios by preventing cryptic failures and data loss. Creates structured error objects, logging infrastructure, state preservation, and recovery mechanisms. This cross-cutting concern impacts 7 scenarios involving Interface and establishes patterns for future error handling. Addresses the #1 hotspot component (Interface) and transforms error handling from afterthought to architectural principle. Enables better diagnosis (S-12) and user trust.

recommendations.recommendation: Design and document plugin/extension API with standardized data exchange contracts and registration mechanisms
recommendations.rationale: Unblocks S-02 (statistical libraries) and S-11 (new model types), enabling third-party integration and system evolution without core modifications. Establishes architectural patterns for extensibility that prevent future scenarios from requiring widespread changes. Affects Linker (#3 hotspot) and Interface (#1 hotspot). Creates clear extension points, versioning strategies, and security boundaries. This transforms the architecture from closed to open for innovation while maintaining core stability. Quick documentation and design phase enables incremental implementation.

recommendations.recommendation: Enforce centralized file I/O through FileManager with explicit architectural constraint that all components route file operations through this layer
recommendations.rationale: Critical for S-09 (encryption compliance) success and improves S-02, S-05, S-10 by creating single control point for security, monitoring, and data exchange. Prevents components from bypassing FileManager, ensuring encryption, access logging, and format standardization work as intended. Addresses architectural ambiguity that creates security gaps. Medium effort to audit and refactor existing direct file access, but establishes principle that prevents future violations. Enables compliance with HIPAA/GDPR requirements.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement performance instrumentation framework at all component boundaries with configurable diagnostic modes
recommendations.rationale: Directly addresses S-12 (4-hour performance diagnosis) and provides visibility into S-03 (error contexts), S-08 (compiler impact), S-10 (resource usage), and S-11 (new model performance). Creates logging infrastructure with timestamps, resource measurements, and profiling hooks. Affects all components but can be implemented incrementally starting with highest-value boundaries (Kernel wrapper, Interface). Transforms reactive performance troubleshooting into proactive monitoring. Enables data-driven optimization decisions.

recommendations.recommendation: Create comprehensive installation and deployment framework with dependency management, version compatibility checking, and automated configuration
recommendations.rationale: Essential for S-05 (50-installation rollout) and improves S-04 (updates), S-08 (compiler dependencies), and S-11 (library distribution). Addresses third-party DLL licensing, registry configuration, and centralized management needs. Prevents deployment failures and version conflicts across user base. Uses standard Windows Installer (MSI) technology with merge modules for components. Medium effort investment prevents high operational costs and support burden. Enables scalable enterprise deployment.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement process isolation architecture where Kernel runs in separate process with IPC communication to Interface
recommendations.rationale: Provides crash isolation for S-03 (error recovery without UI restart), simplifies S-08 (compiler changes isolated to Kernel process), enables S-10 (per-process resource monitoring), and improves S-12 (cleaner profiling boundaries). Transforms catastrophic Kernel failures into recoverable errors. Creates natural extension point for future distributed computing or cloud deployment. Higher initial effort but significantly improves robustness and modifiability. Alternative to in-process wrapper approach with better fault isolation.

recommendations.recommendation: Document architectural decision to remain single-user desktop application and establish criteria for potential client-server redesign
recommendations.rationale: Addresses S-10 architectural mismatch explicitly rather than forcing inappropriate modifications. Clarifies that multi-user server monitoring requires fundamental redesign, not incremental changes. Establishes decision framework: if >X% of scenarios require client-server (currently 1/12), or if stakeholder priorities shift to enterprise deployment, trigger architecture re-evaluation. Prevents wasted effort on half-measures while keeping option open for future pivot. Low effort documentation prevents scope creep.

recommendations.recommendation: Clarify and document Domain Library extension mechanism with explicit distinction between template-based and algorithm-based model types
recommendations.rationale: Resolves S-11 ambiguity about whether new models require Kernel changes (HIGH risk) or configuration (MEDIUM risk). Creates clear guidelines for library authors and establishes boundaries for what's achievable through extension vs. core modification. Affects DomainLibs (#6 hotspot, lowest priority) but prevents future scenarios from having unclear risk profiles. Low-effort documentation and schema definition enables informed decision-making for new model requests. Can be combined with plugin API recommendation for comprehensive extensibility story.
